flashcards.front: Define the complexity class P (Polynomial Time).
flashcards.back: P is the class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time O(n^k) for some constant k. Problems in P are considered efficiently tractable, e.g., sorting, shortest path, and primality testing.

flashcards.front: Define the complexity class NP (Nondeterministic Polynomial Time).
flashcards.back: NP is the class of decision problems where given a certificate (solution), verification can be done in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine. It includes problems like SAT, Clique, and Vertex Cover.

flashcards.front: What is an NP-Complete problem? Give two examples.
flashcards.back: NP-Complete problems are those in NP and every problem in NP can be polynomial-time reducible to them. If any NP-Complete problem is solved in P, then P=NP. Examples: Boolean Satisfiability (SAT), Clique, Vertex Cover, Hamiltonian Cycle, Traveling Salesman Problem (decision version).

flashcards.front: Define NP-Hard problems. How do they differ from NP-Complete?
flashcards.back: NP-Hard problems are at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP (every NP problem reduces to them), but they may not be in NP themselves (solutions may not be verifiable in polynomial time). NP-Complete ⊂ NP-Hard; NP-Hard includes problems not in NP like Halting Problem and Traveling Salesman Optimization.

The Cook-Levin Theorem proves that the Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) problem is NP-Complete. It establishes that SAT is the first NP-Complete problem by showing any problem in NP can be reduced to SAT in polynomial time, providing the foundation for proving NP-Completeness of other problems via reduction from SAT.

flashcards.front: What is polynomial-time reduction? Why is it important?
flashcards.back: Polynomial-time reduction is a transformation from problem A to problem B such that: (1) any instance of A can be transformed to an instance of B in O(n^k) time, and (2) the answer is preserved. It is crucial for proving NP-Completeness—if A ≤p B and B ∈ P, then A ∈ P.

flashcards.front: Compare P, NP, NP-Complete, and NP-Hard with a Venn diagram description.
flashcards.back: P ⊆ NP. NP-Complete is the intersection of NP and NP-Hard. All NP-Complete problems are in NP but NP-Hard may not be. If P ≠ NP (widely believed), then NP-Complete ∩ P = ∅. The relationship: P ⊂ NP-Complete ⊂ NP-Hard, with NP-Hard containing problems harder than any in NP.

flashcards.front: Explain the Vertex Cover problem and its NP-Completeness.
flashcards.back: Vertex Cover: Given graph G and integer k, does G have a set of ≤k vertices touching every edge? It is NP-Complete because: (1) verification is polynomial (check k vertices cover all edges), and (2) reduce from Clique or SAT. Finding minimum Vertex Cover is NP-Hard (optimization version).
